title: Find the Duplicate Number
description: Given an array of integers nums containing n + 1 integers where each integer is in the range [1, n] inclusive, there is only one repeated number in nums. Return this repeated number. You must solve the problem without modifying the array nums and uses only constant extra space.
constraints: All the integers in nums appear only once except for precisely one integer which appears two or more times
thinking_questions: Values are in range [1,n] and indices are [0,n] — if you treat each value as a 'next pointer', what structure does that create?
If there's a duplicate, what does that mean in terms of the 'linked list' created by using values as indices?
Isn't this just cycle detection on a virtual linked list?

hints.L1: Think of each nums[i] as a 'pointer' to index nums[i]. The duplicate value creates two pointers into the same node — meaning a cycle.
hints.L2: Floyd's cycle detection on the virtual linked list (i → nums[i]). Phase 1: find meeting point. Phase 2: find cycle start (the duplicate).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: treat nums as linked list i→nums[i]; duplicate = cycle entry
# Phase 1: find intersection
slow, fast = nums[0], nums[nums[0]]
while slow != fast:
    slow = nums[slow]
    fast = nums[nums[fast]]
# Phase 2: find cycle entry (duplicate)
slow2 = 0
while slow != slow2:
    slow = nums[slow]
    slow2 = nums[slow2]
return slow
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(1)  — cannot modify array, without extra space

follow_up_questions: Explain why the duplicate creates a cycle in the virtual linked list.
Why can't you just sort or use a HashSet here (given the constraints)?
Explain Floyd's phase 2 — why does starting a second slow pointer from 0 find the duplicate?
solution_notes: Floyd's cycle detection on virtual linked list (index→value). Phase 1: find meeting. Phase 2: from 0 find entry=duplicate. O(n), O(1).
